label: tetlock-est-prospective-policy-mitigation
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**What model policies or guidance exist for California public agencies restricting elected officials' use of ephemeral-messaging apps and personal devices for public business, and would adopting such controls now meaningfully mitigate (though not cure) the city's exposure here and in future requests?**

What would have to be true: Identifiable sources (CA AG guidance, League of California Cities model records policies, other agencies' post-San Jose device/retention policies) would need to prescribe concrete measures — e.g., bans on disappearing-message apps for public business, mandatory personal-device search/preservation protocols — and those measures would need to be shown legally sufficient under CPRA/San Jose without themselves creating new Brown Act exposure.
text: {"key_considerations":["San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) itself contemplates agencies using reasonable search methods (e.g., employee declarations/self-certification) for personal-account records rather than mandating forensic seizure of personal devices — this is the doctrinal baseline any 'model policy' must track, and it is well-established, giving confidence that legally-grounded search protocols exist to draw on.","General 'personal device/personal account CPRA policy' templates (from the League of California Cities, its Institute for Local Government, CSAC, and individual city attorney offices) proliferated after San Jose and are reasonably well-attested in CA municipal practice — but verify exact titles/dates/URLs, since I cannot pinpoint a specific document with certainty.","Guidance specifically targeting ephemeral/disappearing-message apps (Signal, auto-delete WhatsApp, etc.) for elected officials is a narrower and newer subset of this guidance; I have lower confidence that mature, CA-specific model policies explicitly addressing auto-delete apps (as opposed to generic 'use your personal phone for city business' policies) are widely published — this is the weakest link in the claim and most needs verification.","'Meaningful mitigation, though not cure' is a legal-strategic judgment, not a factual one: adopting a policy now cannot restore destroyed records or undo a completed Brown Act serial-communication violation, and courts/the public will likely view post-litigation policy adoption skeptically for backward-looking remedies (fees under Gov. Code § 6259(d), adverse inference), though it plausibly carries real weight for prospective injunctive relief, future-request compliance, and settlement posture.","A well-drafted device/retention policy (search protocol, prohibition on ephemeral apps for public business, mandatory forwarding to city systems) is unlikely to itself generate new Brown Act exposure, since Brown Act risk stems from how officials communicate serially about pending business, not from a records-retention/device rule — so that half of the compound claim is comparatively low-risk and likely satisfiable with competent drafting."],"probability":60,"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Would move up with: (1) a located, citable League of California Cities/ILG/CSAC model policy or sample resolution explicitly addressing ephemeral-messaging apps and personal-device search protocols post-San Jose; (2) post-San Jose CA appellate or trial-court authority crediting adoption of such a policy as mitigating evidence for fees, injunctive scope, or good-faith findings; (3) confirmation of any CA AG guidance or legislative amendment (e.g., check whether SB 1189 (2021) or similar extends to local elected officials' personal accounts, not just state agencies). Would move down if research shows existing guidance is only generic (personal email/text, pre-dating ephemeral-app proliferation) with no CA municipal authority yet addressing Signal-type auto-delete tools, or if case law treats post-hoc policy adoption as legally irrelevant to any pending remedy."}

key_considerations: San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) itself contemplates agencies using reasonable search methods (e.g., employee declarations/self-certification) for personal-account records rather than mandating forensic seizure of personal devices — this is the doctrinal baseline any 'model policy' must track, and it is well-established, giving confidence that legally-grounded search protocols exist to draw on.

General 'personal device/personal account CPRA policy' templates (from the League of California Cities, its Institute for Local Government, CSAC, and individual city attorney offices) proliferated after San Jose and are reasonably well-attested in CA municipal practice — but verify exact titles/dates/URLs, since I cannot pinpoint a specific document with certainty.

Guidance specifically targeting ephemeral/disappearing-message apps (Signal, auto-delete WhatsApp, etc.) for elected officials is a narrower and newer subset of this guidance; I have lower confidence that mature, CA-specific model policies explicitly addressing auto-delete apps (as opposed to generic 'use your personal phone for city business' policies) are widely published — this is the weakest link in the claim and most needs verification.

'Meaningful mitigation, though not cure' is a legal-strategic judgment, not a factual one: adopting a policy now cannot restore destroyed records or undo a completed Brown Act serial-communication violation, and courts/the public will likely view post-litigation policy adoption skeptically for backward-looking remedies (fees under Gov. Code § 6259(d), adverse inference), though it plausibly carries real weight for prospective injunctive relief, future-request compliance, and settlement posture.

A well-drafted device/retention policy (search protocol, prohibition on ephemeral apps for public business, mandatory forwarding to city systems) is unlikely to itself generate new Brown Act exposure, since Brown Act risk stems from how officials communicate serially about pending business, not from a records-retention/device rule — so that half of the compound claim is comparatively low-risk and likely satisfiable with competent drafting.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Would move up with: (1) a located, citable League of California Cities/ILG/CSAC model policy or sample resolution explicitly addressing ephemeral-messaging apps and personal-device search protocols post-San Jose; (2) post-San Jose CA appellate or trial-court authority crediting adoption of such a policy as mitigating evidence for fees, injunctive scope, or good-faith findings; (3) confirmation of any CA AG guidance or legislative amendment (e.g., check whether SB 1189 (2021) or similar extends to local elected officials' personal accounts, not just state agencies). Would move down if research shows existing guidance is only generic (personal email/text, pre-dating ephemeral-app proliferation) with no CA municipal authority yet addressing Signal-type auto-delete tools, or if case law treats post-hoc policy adoption as legally irrelevant to any pending remedy.
